specification.topic-statement: The agent navigation subsystem maintains an ordered array of active agents, tracks the currently visible agent, and provides tree-based navigation (parent, sibling, and child) to move between agents.

scope.in-scope: The agent array (add, lookup by UUID, remove), the current agent pointer, tree navigation (to parent, previous sibling, next sibling, most recent child), per-agent state preservation across switches, dead-agent handling, and the reap operation that removes dead agents.
scope.boundary: How a new child agent is created and inserted into the array is covered by the agent fork specification.
How an agent is marked dead in the database is covered by the inter-agent messaging specification.
How agent switch events are triggered by user input is covered by the REPL event loop and command dispatch specifications.

behavior.agent-array:
All active agents are stored in a single dynamically-sized pointer array within the REPL context. The array starts empty and grows by doubling capacity when an insertion would exceed it (initial expansion is to 4 slots).
Agents are stored in insertion order. The array is not sorted; linear scans are used for all lookups.
behavior.add-operation: An agent is added to the end of the array. If the array is at capacity, it is reallocated with doubled capacity before insertion.
behavior.lookup-operation: An agent is located by UUID string. Lookup accepts both full UUIDs and prefix strings (minimum four characters). Exact matches take priority over prefix matches. If multiple agents share a prefix (ambiguous), the lookup returns no result.
behavior.remove-operation: An agent is removed by UUID. The matching entry is located by linear scan, removed, and the remaining entries are shifted to fill the gap. If the removed agent was the current agent, the current pointer is set to null.
behavior.current-agent:
The REPL maintains a single pointer to the currently visible agent. Only one agent is shown to the user at a time. The scrollback, input buffer, and viewport state displayed are those of the current agent.
Each agent stores its own input buffer and viewport scroll offset independently. Switching agents automatically restores whatever state that agent was in when last viewed — no explicit save or restore is needed.
behavior.tree-navigation:
Navigation moves the current pointer according to parent-child relationships derived from each agent's stored parent UUID:
- **To parent**: finds the agent whose UUID matches the current agent's parent UUID. No-op if the current agent has no parent (it is the root). - **To previous sibling**: collects all siblings (agents with the same parent as the current agent), locates the current agent in that list, and moves to the preceding entry, wrapping around to the last sibling. - **To next sibling**: same as previous, but moves to the following entry with wraparound to the first sibling. - **To most recent child**: collects all direct children of the current agent, sorts them by creation timestamp, and moves to the most recently created one. No-op if the current agent has no children.
When collecting siblings or children for navigation, the array is limited to 64 entries. If an agent has more than 64 siblings or children, the navigation considers only the first 64.
behavior.dead-agent-handling:
Killing an agent marks it as dead (in the database and with an in-memory flag) but does not immediately remove it from the array. The killed agent remains visible and navigable until it is explicitly reaped.
If the user is viewing a dead agent, they continue to see it. The scrollback displays a system message indicating the kill. The user can navigate away and the agent remains in place until reaped.
behavior.reap-operation:
The reap operation removes all dead agents from the array. Before removal, if the current agent is among those being reaped (or has an ancestor being reaped), the current agent is switched to the first living (non-dead) agent in the array.
Dead agents and their descendants are identified, removed from the array in a single pass, and their memory is freed. Each reaped agent is also marked as reaped in the database (preserving the database row for audit purposes while removing it from the live set).
When an agent is reaped, all of its descendants are also reaped, regardless of whether they are individually marked dead. This prevents orphaned child agents from remaining in the array when their parent is removed.
